feedback.text: Hello my name is Ferenc Uj, There is a really important issue I would like you to include. There is a practice when an electric or digital good is sold as a perpetual license to the product. This provides a way for the seller to sell seemingly a good without providing any actual ownership to the consumer. The "Switch 2" is an example for how extreme this practice can get. While the purchase in practice does not differ from a normal purchase, in actuality you lose all ownership from the product you paid for. This is thanks to them selling "only a license", which is in reality is a normal product designed to be revoke-able after purchase. For example the "Switch 2" was designed to stop functioning in case of the license being terminated. While its currently only used as an anti-tempering tool, it is concerning, especially since many Terms of Services include forced arbitration clauses and lines such as "This license can be terminated for any reason". In some extreme cases such as in Blizzards' Terms of Service it is even included that the license can be terminated for no reason at all. As long as it is possible for the makers of these products to sell them as licenses, they have no reason to sell them as products. Especially since they can force extremely hostile Terms of Use on us, consumers. They can even destroy products they sold to us in order to force the consumer to buy a new one. For example, there is no real reason for the manufacturer to sell an electric toothbrush as a license to a toothbrush. But doing so would give full control to the manufacturer over the product, even after purchase. They can add a network connection as a requirement to any type of product so when they want the product to stop functioning, they can just simply shut down the authentication services, making them unusable. This is extremely prevalent in the video game industry. Forcing them to have an end of life plan would probably solve most of the issues. In short: There are Terms of Services that are extremely hostile to consumers and should be regulated. Products are sold as licenses to force Terms of Services on consumers and to deny ownership over the product. Many of these designed to stop functioning when the manufacturer/publisher decides them to.
